title: Resource Management: Energy
examTip: For energy management, know the UK's energy transition (offshore wind, Hinkley Point C, net zero 2050) AND a sustainable LIC/NEE case study (Chambamontera micro-hydro or Bangladesh solar). Always evaluate renewable energy — intermittency is the main criticism.
introTips: Know the global energy mix percentages: fossil fuels ~80%, renewables ~11%, nuclear ~5%.
Chambamontera micro-hydro (Peru) or Bangladesh solar home systems are the small-scale LIC case studies.
UK energy: offshore wind ~30% of electricity in 2023; Hinkley Point C is controversial — delayed and over budget.
intro: Almost everything in modern life runs on energy — but 770 million people still have no access to electricity. Meanwhile, burning fossil fuels is driving climate change, and the reserves we depend on are finite. Managing the shift from fossil fuels to renewables is one of the defining political and economic challenges of our time — and it's happening at different speeds in different parts of the world. This lesson covers why energy insecurity exists and how both large-scale and small-scale solutions are being tried.

keyPoints.heading: Global energy demand and the energy mix
keyPoints.content: Global energy demand has roughly tripled since 1965, driven by population growth and economic development (especially in China, India and Africa). The global energy mix currently relies heavily on fossil fuels: coal provides approximately 27%, oil 33%, natural gas 24% — a total of around 80% from fossil fuels. Nuclear provides about 5% and renewables approximately 11% (and growing rapidly). Energy consumption per person varies enormously: the USA uses approximately 15 kilowatts per person continuously; sub-Saharan Africa just 0.6 kilowatts. Around 770 million people have no access to electricity at all (2022), mostly in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Energy security is the ability of a country to access reliable, affordable and ideally sustainable energy supplies. Countries with domestic fossil fuel reserves or large renewable potential have higher energy security; countries that import most of their energy are more vulnerable.
writeBullets: Global demand tripled since 1965 — population growth + economic development
Energy mix: coal 27%, oil 33%, gas 24% — total fossil fuels ~80%
Renewables ~11% globally — growing rapidly; nuclear ~5%
USA: ~15kW per person; sub-Saharan Africa: ~0.6kW per person
770m people have no electricity — mostly sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia
Energy security — reliable, affordable, ideally sustainable supply
keyPoints.memTip: 80% fossil, 11% renewable — we're still overwhelmingly dependent on fuels that will run out.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often underestimate how dominant fossil fuels still are — ~80% globally. Renewables are growing fast but from a small base.

keyPoints.heading: Causes of energy insecurity
keyPoints.content: Energy insecurity has multiple causes. Finite fossil fuels: estimated reserves at current use rates are approximately 133 years for coal, 50 years for oil, and 50 years for gas — though new discoveries and technology (fracking, deep sea drilling) complicate these estimates. Geopolitical factors: the Middle East holds the majority of the world's oil reserves, making global supply vulnerable to regional instability. The 1973 OPEC oil embargo raised oil prices by 400%, causing the first global energy crisis and revealing how dependent Western countries were on Middle Eastern oil. More recently, Russia's role as Europe's major gas supplier was exposed in 2022 when the Russia-Ukraine war caused gas supplies to be cut, sending European energy prices to record highs. Economic factors: price volatility makes energy planning very difficult for LICs with limited financial reserves. Growing demand from India, China and Africa adds pressure. Environmental conflict: the need to decarbonise means we can't just burn more coal to fix short-term energy shortages without worsening long-term climate change.
writeBullets: Finite fossil fuels — coal ~133 years, oil ~50 years, gas ~50 years at current use
Middle East geopolitics — majority of oil reserves; instability disrupts supply
OPEC oil embargo 1973 — prices rose 400%; first global energy crisis
Russia-Ukraine war 2022 — gas supply cuts; European prices hit record highs
Price volatility — makes planning difficult, especially for LICs
Growing demand — China, India, Africa adding pressure
Decarbonisation conflict — can't solve energy shortage by burning more coal
keyPoints.memTip: FGEPGD — Finite, Geopolitics, Economic volatility, Population, Growing demand, Decarbonisation.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The 1973 oil embargo AND the 2022 Russia-Ukraine war are both excellent geopolitical examples — use both to show the problem is not new.

keyPoints.heading: Renewable energy sources
keyPoints.content: Solar: photovoltaic (PV) cells convert sunlight directly into electricity; solar thermal systems heat water. The cost of solar PV has dropped 90% in 10 years, making it the fastest-growing energy source globally. Wind: onshore wind is the cheapest source of new electricity (around £80/MWh); offshore is more reliable and less controversial. The UK is a world leader — Hornsea Wind Farm off the Yorkshire coast is the world's largest offshore wind farm (1.3 GW capacity). Hydroelectric power (HEP): reliable and fuel-free once built — but requires a large river and often a large dam. Three Gorges Dam (22.5 GW, displaced 1.3 million people); run-of-river schemes cause less disruption. Tidal: highly predictable (tides are reliable) — the Swansea Bay tidal lagoon proposal has been discussed but not built due to cost. Geothermal: uses heat from within the Earth — Iceland gets 100% of its electricity from renewables using geothermal and HEP; UK potential is limited. Biomass: burning organic material (wood, crops) — controversial as it emits CO₂, competes with food production for land, and raises biodiversity concerns. Drax power station in Yorkshire uses biomass.
writeBullets: Solar PV — cost down 90% in 10 years; fastest-growing energy source globally
Wind — onshore ~£80/MWh; Hornsea offshore wind farm = world's largest (1.3 GW, Yorkshire)
HEP — reliable, no fuel; Three Gorges 22.5 GW but displaced 1.3m people
Tidal — very predictable; Swansea Bay lagoon proposed but not built (too expensive)
Geothermal — Iceland 100% renewables from geothermal and HEP; UK potential limited
Biomass — Drax (Yorkshire); burning organic material; CO₂ emissions; land use controversy
keyPoints.memTip: SWHTGB — Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Geo, Biomass. Six renewables; know one example each.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Hornsea is the UK offshore wind flagship example — world's largest at 1.3 GW. Learn it.

keyPoints.heading: The UK's energy transition
keyPoints.content: The UK's electricity mix has changed dramatically in the past decade. In 2023, the UK generated approximately: wind ~30%, gas ~32%, nuclear ~15%, solar ~5%, other renewables ~8%. North Sea oil and gas production peaked in 1999 and is now declining significantly, though it still provides substantial domestic supply. Government targets include offshore wind expansion to 40 GW by 2030 (from about 14 GW in 2023); new nuclear at Hinkley Point C (Somerset — but significantly delayed and over budget, now projected to cost £35+ billion); a hydrogen strategy; and incentives for heat pumps to replace gas boilers. Energy efficiency is also key: UK housing is among Europe's worst-insulated, contributing to high bills; LED lighting, smart meters and electric vehicles are all part of the transition. Energy poverty: 13% of UK households were in fuel poverty in 2023 — unable to afford adequate heat. The UK's legally binding target is to reach net zero by 2050.
writeBullets: UK 2023 electricity: wind ~30%, gas ~32%, nuclear ~15%, solar ~5%, other renewables ~8%
North Sea — peaked 1999; declining but still significant domestic supply
Offshore wind target — 40 GW by 2030; UK world leader in offshore wind
Hinkley Point C — new nuclear; severely delayed; cost rising to £35bn+
Energy poverty — 13% of UK households in fuel poverty (2023)
Net zero target — legally binding; 2050 deadline
UK housing poorly insulated — major obstacle to efficiency; heat pumps to replace gas
keyPoints.memTip: UK: wind up, gas still dominant, nuclear expensive, net zero by 2050. Progress but long way to go.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Hinkley Point C is a nuanced example — it's the government's flagship new nuclear, but it's massively over budget and delayed. Good for evaluation.

keyPoints.heading: Sustainable energy in LICs and NEEs
keyPoints.content: Small-scale renewable energy can transform lives in remote communities. Chambamontera, Peru (Cajamarca region, Andes) — a remote mountain community with no grid access received a micro-hydro scheme installed by Practical Action NGO: a small turbine in a local stream generates 1 kW of electricity. Impact: electric lighting extended productive hours; a refrigerator stores medicines for the health post; the school uses computers; small businesses have developed (phone charging services). The whole system cost approximately £4,000 and is managed by the community. This approach has been scaled: over 10,000 micro-hydro schemes operate across Peru. Bangladesh solar home systems (Grameen Shakti programme): over 4 million solar panels have been installed in rural homes, with more than 2 million systems by 2018. The programme reduces kerosene use (saving money and cutting indoor air pollution), creates local employment through trained solar technicians, and reduces carbon emissions. Both examples show that energy solutions for LICs can be appropriate technology — simple, affordable, community-managed and locally maintained.
writeBullets: Chambamontera, Peru — micro-hydro scheme; 1 kW turbine; £4,000 total cost
Impact: lighting, medicine refrigeration, school computers, small businesses
Managed by local community; 10,000+ micro-hydro schemes across Peru
Bangladesh solar (Grameen Shakti) — 4m+ solar panels; 2m+ systems by 2018
Bangladesh: reduces kerosene, cuts indoor air pollution, trains local technicians
Both are appropriate technology — simple, affordable, community-managed
keyPoints.memTip: £4,000 micro-hydro in Peru + 4m solar panels in Bangladesh = appropriate technology in action.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Chambamontera is a powerful small-scale case study — the cost (£4,000), the output (1 kW) and the community impact together paint a complete picture.

rows: Solar PV
Sunlight converted to electricity by photovoltaic cells
Cost fallen 90% in 10 years; no fuel; works anywhere with sunshine
Intermittent (no sun at night/cloudy days); large land area needed
Solar farms in south England; domestic roof panels
Offshore wind
Wind turns turbines generating electricity
Reliable winds; no visual impact on land; cheapest new electricity source
Intermittent (no wind sometimes); expensive installation; affects seabirds
Hornsea Wind Farm, Yorkshire — world's largest (1.3 GW)
HEP
Falling water spins turbines via a dam or run-of-river
Very reliable; no fuel cost once built; long lifespan
Requires large dam; displaces communities; changes ecosystems
Dinorwig pump-storage, Wales
Geothermal
Heat from Earth's interior used for electricity or heating
Reliable; runs 24/7; no fuel; low emissions
Only viable in volcanic regions; high drilling costs
Iceland (100% renewables); limited UK potential
Biomass
Burning organic material (wood, crops) for electricity or heat
Carbon neutral if managed; can use waste material
CO₂ emissions at point of burning; land use competes with food; biodiversity concerns
Drax power station, Yorkshire

1.question: Why did the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022 cause an energy security crisis in Europe?
options: Russia reduced gas supplies to Europe, causing prices to reach record highs as countries depended on Russian gas
The war destroyed Ukraine's nuclear power stations, reducing European electricity supply
Sanctions against Russia blocked European oil companies from drilling in the North Sea
Fighting disrupted European wind farm construction, causing a renewable energy shortage
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Russia was a major gas supplier to Europe — when it cut supplies in 2022 following the invasion of Ukraine, European countries scrambled for alternative supplies and prices hit record highs. This exposed how dependent some European countries had become on a single geopolitical supplier.
1.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Russia cut gas supplies to Europe in 2022, causing European energy prices to hit record highs. Many European countries had become heavily dependent on Russian gas — the war revealed this as a major energy security vulnerability.

4.question: What makes the Chambamontera micro-hydro scheme in Peru an example of sustainable energy development?
options: It is community-managed, uses local stream power, cost only ~£4,000, and has created lighting, medical refrigeration and small businesses
It is a large-scale scheme transferring water from the Amazon to power turbines across Peru at low cost
It was built by the Peruvian government with Chinese investment as part of an infrastructure programme
It uses geothermal energy from the Andes volcanoes to generate electricity for the whole Cajamarca region
4.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Chambamontera is a textbook example of appropriate technology and sustainable development: a £4,000 micro-hydro turbine in a local stream, managed by the community, that has transformed lives through lighting, medicine storage, school computers and new businesses.
4.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Chambamontera is a micro-hydro scheme — a small turbine in a local stream installed by Practical Action NGO for ~£4,000. It's community-managed and has provided lighting, medicine refrigeration and school computers. That's what makes it sustainable.

commonMistakes: Saying renewables already dominate global energy — fossil fuels still provide ~80% of global energy; renewables are ~11% and growing, but are not yet dominant at global scale.
Confusing Hornsea Wind Farm (offshore, world's largest, 1.3 GW) with Hinkley Point C (nuclear power station — different technology entirely).
Saying renewable energy has no environmental impact — wind farms affect seabirds and landscapes; HEP dams displace communities; biomass releases CO₂. Always mention trade-offs.
Forgetting energy poverty — 13% of UK households in fuel poverty (2023); energy insecurity is not just a problem for LICs; it exists within HICs too.
Not evaluating Hinkley Point C — it's the UK's new nuclear flagship but it is massively delayed and over budget; this is important critical evaluation material.
examTips: For renewable energy questions, use the SWHTGB mnemonic (Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Geo, Biomass) and aim to evaluate at least two types — discussing both advantages AND disadvantages.

When writing about the UK energy transition, always mention net zero 2050, the offshore wind target (40 GW by 2030), and the energy poverty problem (13%) to show awareness of both ambition and challenge.
Chambamontera and the Bangladesh solar scheme are both examples of appropriate technology in LICs — use them together to show a pattern, not just one isolated example.
For extended writing, contrast small-scale appropriate technology (Chambamontera: £4,000, community-managed) with large-scale (offshore wind, Hinkley C) to analyse the trade-offs of scale vs impact.
revisionCardBullets: Global energy: coal 27%, oil 33%, gas 24% — fossil fuels ~80% total
Renewables ~11% globally; 770m people have no electricity
OPEC oil embargo 1973 — prices +400%; Russia-Ukraine 2022 — gas supply cuts
Solar PV — cost down 90% in 10 years; fastest-growing source
Hornsea Wind Farm, Yorkshire — world's largest offshore wind; 1.3 GW
Iceland — 100% renewables from geothermal and HEP
Hinkley Point C — new nuclear; delayed; cost rising to £35bn+
UK net zero target — legally binding; 2050
Chambamontera, Peru — micro-hydro; £4,000; 1 kW; community-managed; lighting + medicine + business
Bangladesh Grameen Shakti — 4m+ solar panels; reduces kerosene; local technician training
